overview.whyItMatters: Vector stores are the secret sauce behind RAG systems. Without a good one, your AI can't find the information it needs to give accurate answers. Pick the right store and your RAG app feels magical. Pick the wrong one and you'll either drown in complexity or hit walls when you need advanced features. Understanding which vector store fits each scenario is essential for building effective RAG applications.

concepts.title: How Vector Search Works
concepts.content:
Forget everything you know about traditional search. When you search Google for 'best pizza near me,' it's matching keywords. Vector search is completely different—it finds things with similar *meaning*, even if they use totally different words.

Here's the magic trick: **embeddings**. Your AI converts text into a list of 1536 numbers (for Titan Embeddings). These numbers position your text in a massive mathematical space. The phrase 'automobile repair' ends up near 'car mechanic' in this space—not because they share words, but because they mean similar things. The embedding model learned these relationships by reading billions of documents.

When someone asks a question, the system converts that question into numbers too. Then it finds the documents whose numbers are closest—these are your most semantically relevant matches.

For speed at scale, vector stores use clever shortcuts. **HNSW** (Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs) creates a layered graph structure that finds approximate nearest neighbors incredibly fast. It might not find the mathematically *perfect* matches, but it finds very close matches in milliseconds instead of seconds. That trade-off is worth it.

**k-NN (k-Nearest Neighbors)** is the algorithm doing the matching—find the k documents most similar to the query. If k=5, you get the 5 closest matches. Simple concept, powerful results.
keyPoints: Embeddings: Text becomes 1536 numbers that capture meaning
Semantic proximity: Similar meanings = close positions in vector space
k-NN: Find the k most similar documents to a query
HNSW: Fast approximate search that trades perfection for speed
concepts.examTip: Titan Embeddings = 1536 dimensions. If a question asks about dimension trade-offs: higher = more nuance but more storage/compute.

concepts.title: Vector Store Options in AWS
concepts.content:
You have three main choices, and picking the right one saves you from pain later. Think of it like choosing a car: sometimes you need a minivan, sometimes a sports car, sometimes what you already have in the garage.

**Bedrock Knowledge Bases** is the minivan. Does everything you need, zero maintenance. Point it at your S3 bucket, pick an embedding model, and you're done. Bedrock handles the messy parts: parsing PDFs and Word docs, chunking them into pieces, generating embeddings, storing vectors, and keeping everything synced when documents change. Most RAG projects should start here.

**OpenSearch Service** is the sports car. More power, more control, more work. OpenSearch gives you **hybrid search**—combining keyword matching with vector similarity. This combo often beats either approach alone. You also get advanced filtering, custom scoring, and fine control over sharding. Use OpenSearch when Bedrock KB's features aren't enough or when you're dealing with billions of vectors.

**Aurora pgvector** is using what's already in the garage. If your team already uses PostgreSQL, adding vectors to the same database simplifies your architecture. You query vectors with familiar SQL syntax. Join vector results with your relational data. No new database to learn. But it lacks some bells and whistles that dedicated vector databases offer.

The most common mistake? Choosing OpenSearch 'just in case' when Bedrock KB would be plenty. That's like buying a sports car for grocery runs—you're paying for complexity you don't need.
keyPoints: Bedrock KB: Zero infrastructure, automatic sync from S3—your default choice
OpenSearch: Hybrid search, advanced filtering, fine-grained control
Aurora pgvector: SQL integration for teams already using PostgreSQL
DynamoDB: Good for metadata, but NOT for vector search itself
concepts.examTip: 'Simplest' or 'fully managed' → Bedrock KB. 'Hybrid search' → OpenSearch. 'Existing PostgreSQL' → Aurora pgvector.

concepts.title: Document Chunking Strategies
concepts.content:
Here's a problem nobody warns you about: you can't just throw a 100-page document at an embedding model. Models have limits, and even if they didn't, a single embedding for an entire book would be too vague to match specific queries. Solution? Chop documents into smaller chunks.

**Fixed-size chunking** is the straightforward approach: split every N tokens with configurable overlap. Bedrock KB defaults to **512 max tokens** with **20% overlap**. Great for dev/testing where you want fast iteration. The downside? You might slice a sentence in half. A chunk that starts with 'However, the alternative...' makes no sense without context.

**Semantic chunking** is smarter—it uses an embedding model to analyze relationships within text and splits at natural meaning boundaries. You configure: **max buffer size** (surrounding sentences to consider—1 means current + one before/after), **max token size** (20-8,192 tokens per chunk), and **breakpoint threshold** (95% recommended—similarity threshold for splitting). **Important**: Semantic chunking costs extra because it runs an FM during ingestion. Best for uniformly dense prose documents.

**Hierarchical chunking** is the production powerhouse—and often the **recommended default**. It creates **parent chunks** (~1000 tokens) and **child chunks** (~500 tokens) with overlap between them (~70 tokens). During retrieval, the system finds relevant children but can substitute broader parent chunks for context. Best for complex docs: technical manuals, legal documents, anything with nested structure.

**Overlapping chunks** handle the boundary problem. If your chunks are 512 tokens with 50-token overlap, each chunk shares some content with its neighbors. When an answer spans a chunk boundary, both adjacent chunks contain it.

**CRITICAL EXAM POINT**: You **cannot change chunking strategy after creating a data source**. Choose wisely upfront, or you'll re-ingest everything.

**Production pattern**: Start with **HIERARCHICAL + hybrid search + reranking**. It's the most robust default. Use SEMANTIC only for uniformly dense prose. Use FIXED_SIZE in dev for fast iteration.
keyPoints: Fixed-size: 512 tokens, 20% overlap default—simple, good for dev
Semantic: FM-powered splitting at meaning boundaries—costs extra during ingestion
Hierarchical: Parent (~1000) + child (~500) tokens—RECOMMENDED FOR PRODUCTION
CRITICAL: Chunking strategy CANNOT be changed after data source creation
concepts.examTip: 'Production RAG' or 'complex documents' → hierarchical. 'Cost-conscious' or 'simple docs' → fixed-size. Remember: chunking can't be changed after creation!

concepts.title: Metadata and Filtering
concepts.content:
Pure vector search returns whatever's semantically similar. But what if you need results from a specific department? Or documents the user actually has permission to access? That's where metadata filtering saves the day.

**Metadata** is just extra info attached to each vector—key-value pairs like `{department: 'engineering', date: '2024-01-15', access_level: 'confidential'}`. You decide what to track based on what filters you'll need.

**Pre-filtering** applies constraints before vector search. User asks about engineering policies? First narrow to engineering documents, then find the semantically best matches within that subset. This is efficient because you're searching a smaller pile.

**Post-filtering** runs vector search first, then filters results. Simpler to implement but risky—if many top matches fail the filter, you end up with few results. Works fine when filters eliminate only a small percentage.

**Access control** is the killer use case. Tag documents with access levels, then filter based on who's asking. Users can't retrieve documents they shouldn't see—even if those docs are perfect semantic matches for their query. This isn't optional for most enterprise deployments.

In S3, object metadata and tags provide these attributes. Bedrock KB can extract S3 metadata during ingestion and use it for filtering. For custom implementations, you store metadata alongside vectors in OpenSearch or Aurora.

Plan your metadata schema before you start ingesting documents. Adding fields later usually means re-processing everything—a painful lesson many teams learn the hard way.
keyPoints: Pre-filtering: Constrain before search (efficient for common filters)
Post-filtering: Filter after search (simpler but may reduce results)
Access control: Filter by user permissions—essential for security
Schema planning: Design metadata fields BEFORE bulk ingestion

skillExplanations.explanation:
Choosing your vector database is a big deal—get it wrong and you're either drowning in complexity or hitting walls when you need features.

**Bedrock Knowledge Bases** should be your starting point. Seriously. It handles everything: document parsing, chunking, embedding, storage, and sync. You just point it at S3 and configure a few settings. Most RAG use cases don't need more than this, and the operational simplicity is hard to overstate.

Go with **OpenSearch** when you genuinely need its superpowers. Hybrid search (keyword + vector) often outperforms pure vector search. Advanced filtering with complex boolean logic. Fine-grained sharding control for billion-vector scale. These features matter—but only if you actually need them.

**Aurora pgvector** is the right call when your team already lives in PostgreSQL. Adding vector search to your existing database is simpler than introducing a new data store. You query vectors with SQL you already know. But if you're starting fresh, there's usually no reason to pick Aurora over Bedrock KB.

The anti-pattern to avoid: choosing OpenSearch 'for future flexibility' when Bedrock KB handles your current needs. You're trading operational simplicity for hypothetical benefits.

awsServices.whenToUse: Most RAG apps. Minimal ops burden. Managed parsing, chunking, embedding, sync.
Hybrid search, advanced filtering, large scale, custom scoring.
Existing PostgreSQL infrastructure. SQL-based vector queries.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Metadata turns basic vector search into precise retrieval. Without it, you can only search by meaning. With it, you can constrain results any way you want.

**S3 object metadata** is your foundation for Bedrock Knowledge Bases. When uploading documents, attach metadata properties: content type, author, department, creation date, access level. Bedrock KB extracts this during ingestion and lets you filter on it during retrieval.

Design your metadata schema thoughtfully before bulk ingestion. Ask yourself: What filters will users need? Date ranges? Departments? Document types? Access control? Each required filter needs a metadata field. Adding fields later typically means re-processing all documents—expensive and annoying.

For custom implementations with OpenSearch or Aurora, store metadata alongside vectors. OpenSearch puts metadata in the document body with proper index mappings. Aurora uses relational columns next to the vector column.

**Tag-based classification** enables smart routing. If your knowledge base spans HR, legal, and engineering docs, tag by domain and route queries to the right subset. Better relevance, faster search.

awsServices.whenToUse: Document storage with object metadata and tags. Primary source for Bedrock KB.
Extract and use S3 metadata for filtering during retrieval.

skillExplanations.explanation:
When you're dealing with millions of vectors, indexing strategy determines whether searches take milliseconds or seconds.

**OpenSearch sharding** distributes vectors across multiple nodes. Each shard handles part of the data and query load. More shards = more parallelism, but also more coordination overhead. Size them based on your data volume—oversized shards hurt performance, too many small shards add complexity.

**Multi-index strategies** separate different document types into their own indexes. Your HR docs get one index, technical docs get another, each tuned for its content. Queries route to the right index based on the request, shrinking the search space.

**Hierarchical indexing** creates parent-child relationships. Parents might be section summaries; children contain details. Query parents to find relevant sections, then drill into children. Preserves context that flat indexing loses.

**Index refresh rate** affects how quickly new documents become searchable. More frequent refreshes = faster visibility, but more resource consumption. Balance freshness against cluster load.

For Bedrock Knowledge Bases, all this is handled automatically. OpenSearch and Aurora require you to configure these settings based on your specific needs.

awsServices.whenToUse: Large-scale deployments needing sharding and indexing control. Performance-critical apps.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Knowledge base integration connects your document sources to the vector store and keeps everything synchronized as content changes.

Bedrock Knowledge Bases supports multiple data sources: S3 buckets with documents, Confluence spaces, SharePoint sites, even web crawlers. For each source, configure the connection, specify what to include, and set sync schedules.

**Sync schedules** control how quickly changes appear in your vector store. On-demand syncs happen when you trigger them. Scheduled syncs run automatically—hourly, daily, weekly. For rapidly changing content, sync often. For stable archives, weekly is fine.

**Document structure** affects chunking and retrieval. Well-organized documents with clear headings produce better chunks than walls of text. Consider preprocessing documents to improve structure before ingestion—it can significantly boost retrieval quality.

For custom implementations, design your ingestion pipeline carefully. Lambda functions triggered by S3 events can process new documents, generate embeddings via Bedrock, and insert vectors into OpenSearch or Aurora. Step Functions orchestrate complex multi-stage pipelines with error handling.

Maintain mapping between source documents and their vectors. When you update or delete a source document, you need to update or remove its vectors too. Otherwise your RAG system serves stale information.

awsServices.whenToUse: Integrate S3, Confluence, SharePoint, or web content with managed sync.
Primary document storage and source for knowledge base sync. Event notifications for custom pipelines.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Vector stores go stale without maintenance. Documents get added, modified, deleted—if your vectors don't keep up, your RAG system serves outdated or wrong information.

**Incremental updates** process only what changed, not the entire corpus. Essential for large knowledge bases where full re-indexing would take forever. Track document versions or modification timestamps to identify changes.

**S3 event notifications** are your friend for change detection. Objects get created, modified, or deleted—S3 fires events. Lambda functions respond, processing affected documents and updating the vector store.

Bedrock Knowledge Bases handles incremental updates automatically. The service tracks what's been processed and only handles changes since last sync. You just configure sync schedules or trigger manual syncs.

For custom implementations, **handle deletions carefully**. When a source document disappears, its vectors must go too. Otherwise your RAG might return information from deleted documents—awkward at best, dangerous at worst. Maintain clear mapping between sources and vectors.

**Monitor sync health** with CloudWatch. Failed syncs, processing delays, growing backlogs—these signal problems. Build observability from the start. The worst time to discover your sync is broken is when users complain about stale answers.

awsServices.whenToUse: Process S3 events. Custom sync logic for OpenSearch or Aurora pipelines.
Orchestrate complex sync workflows. Handle failures with retry and fallback logic.
Monitor sync health, processing times, error rates. Alert on failures.

values.Bedrock KB Managed: Most RAG apps, minimal ops overhead
values.OpenSearch Service: Advanced search, large scale, complex filtering
values.Aurora pgvector: Teams with PostgreSQL expertise, combined workloads

examStrategies.approach: Default to Bedrock Knowledge Bases. It handles chunking, embedding, indexing, and sync automatically. Only pick OpenSearch or Aurora when the question explicitly requires features Bedrock KB can't provide.

OpenSearch Neural plugin or Bedrock KB HYBRID mode. Both combine BM25 keywords with vector similarity. If the question emphasizes simplicity, go Bedrock KB HYBRID. If it mentions advanced control, go OpenSearch.
Aurora pgvector integrates vector search into existing PostgreSQL. This is the answer ONLY when the question explicitly mentions existing PostgreSQL—don't assume it otherwise.

keyTakeaways: Bedrock KB is the simplest path to RAG—handles parsing, chunking, embedding, and sync
OpenSearch for hybrid search, advanced filtering, and scale—use when you need these
Aurora pgvector for existing PostgreSQL workloads, not as a default choice
Chunking strategy directly impacts retrieval quality—test different sizes with your data
Plan metadata schema before ingestion—adding fields later means re-processing everything
commonMistakes: Choosing OpenSearch when Bedrock KB would suffice (unnecessary complexity)
Forgetting that Bedrock KB handles chunking automatically
Not using hybrid search when both keywords and semantics matter
Using DynamoDB for vector search (it's for metadata, not vectors)
Skipping metadata planning and re-processing documents later
